content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ego_2">: not emphatic itself, but expressed merely to set off </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vos_2">, which is. The Latin is so fond of putting pronouns in contrast that one is often (as here) expressed for the mere purpose of antithesis. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="judices_2">: not <gloss>judges</gloss>, but rather <gloss>jurors</gloss>. They were persons selected by law to try facts (under the presidency of a <foreign xml:lang="lat">praetor</foreign> or <foreign xml:lang="lat">judex quaestionis</foreign>), and varied in number from a single one to fifty or more. They were originally selected from the Senators, but C. Gracchus had transferred the right to sit as <foreign xml:lang="lat"> judices</foreign> to the <foreign xml:lang="lat">equites</foreign> or wealthy middle class). Sulla, whose reforms went into operation <date when="-0080">B.C. 80</date>, had restored this right to the Senators, and the present case was the first to occur under the new system. It was brought in the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Quaestio inter sicarios</foreign> (or court for the trial of murder), under the presidency of the praetor M. Fannius. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_quod_2"> . <gloss>Why it is that</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_2"> (causal) . . . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="surrexerim_2"> expresses <emph>a fact</emph>, and takes the subj. of informal ind. disc. as depending on the indirect question </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_sit_2">. § <ref n="AG 592">592</ref>, 1 (341, b); cf. B. 323; G. 663.1; H. 652 (529,ii) H-B. 535, I, a. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="summi_nobilissimi_2">: notice the chiastic order: § <ref n="AG 598">598</ref>, f (344, f); B. 350, II, c; G. 682; H. 666, 2 (562) H-B 628 </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_sedeant_2"> : </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_2"> has a slight concessive force: render by <gloss> when</gloss> or <gloss> while</gloss>; <gloss>though</gloss> would be too strong. Since Sulla's victory had restored the aristocracy to power, it might be expected that men of rank (<foreign xml:lang="lat">nobilissimi</foreign>) would have courage to come forward and defend Roscius: their presence showed their sympathies, though they did not rise to defend him. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ego_2_2">: emphatic, as opposed to the orators and men of rank. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="potissimum_2"> , <gloss>rather than any other</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aetate_2">: Cicero was but twenty-six years old. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sim_2">: in direct disc. this might be either subj. to indicate the character of Cicero, or indic. to denote a mere fact about him; here it is necessarily subj. as being an integral part of the clause </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_2_2"> . . . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="surrexerim_2_2">, which is itself dependent on </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_sit_2_2">; § <ref n="AG 593">593</ref> (342); B. 324,1; (G. 663,1; H. 652, I (529, ii); H.-B. 539. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sedeant_2">, <gloss>sit still</gloss>, instead of rising to speak: subj. of integral part, dependent on </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sim_comparandus_2"> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hi_2">: strongly demonstrative; accompanied, perhaps, with a gesture, <gloss>these men here</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="injuriam_2"> ,<gloss>injustice</gloss> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="novo_scelere_2"> (abl. of means), <gloss>the strange</gloss> (almost = unheard of) <gloss> charge</gloss> (of parricide). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="oportere_2">: this verb is always impersonal ; its subject here is the clause </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="injuriam_defendi_2"> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="defendi_2">,</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="defendere_2">: see Vocab.; supply <gloss>but</gloss> (suggested in Latin by the close juxtaposition of the two infs.) before </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="defendere_2_2"> in translating. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="iniquitatem_temporum_2">, i.e. the disturbed state of politics, while the wounds of the Civil War were still fresh. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ita_fit_2">: the subject is the clause </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_adsint_2">, etc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adsint_2">,<gloss>they attend</gloss>: opposed to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="taceant_2">; the position of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="taceant_2_2"> indicates this antithesis. The friends of any party to a suit attended court to give him the advantage of their presence and influence (cf. Caes. B.G. 1. 4). Such friends were technically called <foreign xml:lang="lat">advocati</foreign>, but they did not, like the modern <gloss> advocate</gloss>, speak in court. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="officium_2"> ,<gloss>duty</gloss>, arising from their relations to the murdered man, who had stood in the relation of <gloss>hospitality</gloss> (see <gloss> hospes</gloss> in Vocab.) with some of the highest families. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="audacissimus_2">, i.e. is it that I have more effrontery than any of the rest? </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ne_quidem_2"> ,<gloss>not . . . either</gloss>, endorsing, as usual, the emphatic word: § <ref n="AG 322">322</ref>, f (151, e); B. 151, e; G. 448, N.2; H. (569, iii, 2). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="istius_2">, i.e. that which is in your thoughts: § <ref n="AG 297">297</ref>, c (102, c); B. 87; G. 306; H.507, 3 (450); H.-B. 271, a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sim_2_2">,<foreign xml:lang="lat">conjunctivus modestiae</foreign>: §<ref n="AG 447"> 447</ref>, vi (311, b); Cf. B. 280, 2 ; G. 257,1; H. 556 (486, i); H.-B. 519,1, b. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aliis_2">, dat.: § <ref n="AG 381"> 381</ref> (229); B. 180, 2, d; G. 345; H. 429, 2 (386, 2); H.-B. 371. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praereptam_2">: prae- gives here the force of <gloss>getting the start of others</gloss> in snatching it (cf. <gloss> prevent</gloss>, from <foreign xml:lang="lat">praevenio</foreign>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="me_2">: so emphatic as to throw </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="igitur_2"> out of its usual place. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="reciperem_2"> ,<gloss>undertake</gloss> a case offered; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="suscipere_2"> is to take up of one's own motion. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="amplitudo_2"> ,<gloss>position</gloss>, from birth, wealth, office, or the like. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="id_quod_2">,<gloss>a thing which</gloss>: §<ref n="AG 307">307</ref>, d (200, e); G. 614, R.2; H.399,6 (445, 7); H.-B. 325, a and N.2. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dixisset_2">, an integral part of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="putaretur_2"> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="putaretur_2_2">: apodosis of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fecisset_2">; §<ref n="AG 517">517</ref> (308); B. 304, I; G. 597; H. 579 (510); H.-B. 581. The whole from </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_verbum_2"> through </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="putaret_2"> is the apodosis of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_dixisset_2"> in 1.14. Translate, <gloss>if any one had spoken, in case he had made any allusion to politics, he would</gloss>, etc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ego_3">, etc., <gloss>but in</gloss> MY <gloss>case, even if I</gloss>, etc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="etiamsi_dixero_3">, . . .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="poterit_3">: §<ref n="AG 516">516</ref>, c (307, c); B. 264, a; G. 244, 2; H. 574, 2 (508, 2); cf. H.-B. 494 and 579, a. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="similiter_3"> ,<gloss>in like manner</gloss>, i.e. as if a man of rank had spoken. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="exire_3">, etc., i.e. this speech will not be quoted and talked over, and hence any allusions to politics which it may contain will not seem more significant than they really are. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="emanare_3"> not to be confounded with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="manere_3">. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="deinde_3"> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_3">: the second reason, corresponding to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quia_3"> in 1. 14. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ceterorum_3">, opposed to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ego_3_2"> in 1.6, below. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dictum_3">: noun, limited by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ceterorum_3_2"> ;</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dicto_3"> (l.5) is also a noun, though modified by an adv. ; § <ref n="AG 321"> 321</ref>, b (207, c) ; G. 437, R. ; H.-B. 250, 2, b, N. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="concedi_3">, impersonal: § <ref n="AG 372">372</ref> (230); B. 187, ii, b; G. 217; H.426, 3 (384, 5); H.-B. 364, 2. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nondum_accessi_3">, I have not yet gone into public life, i.e. become candidate for any office. Cicero began his political career five years later, with the quaestorship. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tametsi_3">,<gloss> although</gloss>, in its so-called "corrective" use, — the concession coming after the general statement, as a kind of limitation of it. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ignoscendi_ratio_3"> ,<gloss>the idea of pardon</gloss>. The vaguely general word <foreign xml:lang="lat">ratio</foreign> with the gen. of the gerund expresses little more than our word <gloss> pardoning</gloss> alone. The Latin, being poor in abstract words, has to resort to such shifts as this to supply their place. So </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cognoscendi_consuetudo_3"> ,<gloss>the habit of judicial investigation</gloss>, is almost equivalent to <gloss>judicial investigation</gloss> simply. This was a bold speech to make under the rule of the tyrant Sulla. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="accedit_4">,<gloss>there is in addition</gloss>: used as a kind of passive of <foreign xml:lang="lat">addo</foreign> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illa_4">,<gloss> this</gloss>, i.e. the following (a common use of this pronoun). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_4">,<gloss> that</gloss>: §<ref n="AG 572">572</ref>(333); B. 299,1,b; G. 525, 1; H.588,3 (540, iv); H.-B. 549,550, 552, 1. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="a_ceteris_4"> ,<gloss>from</gloss> <gloss>the others</gloss>, i.e. the nobles. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="petitum_sit_4">: for subjunctive see § <ref n="AG 447">447</ref>, a and N. (334,g and N.) ; G. 457, 2, N.; H. (p.267, footnote 1); H.-B. 517,1. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_dicerent_4"> [<foreign xml:lang="lat"> causam</foreign>], subst. clause of purpose, subj. of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="petitum_sit_4_2">: §<ref n="AG 566">566</ref> (331, h) ; G. 546; H.565, 2 (499, 3); cf. H.-B. 502, 3, a. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dicere_causam_4"> is the technical expression for defending a case. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_arbitrarentur_4">: a clause of result, dependent on </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ita_sit_4">: §<ref n="AG 537">537</ref> and N.2 (319 and a.) ; B. 284, 1; G. 552; H. 591 (500 and N.1); H.-B. 521, 2 and a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="utrumvis_4"> ,<gloss>either</gloss> [course, i.e. to speak or be silent], <gloss>at their choice</gloss> ; lit. <gloss>either</gloss> [of the two] <gloss>you please</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="salvo_officio_4"> (abl. of manner), <gloss>without a breach of duty</gloss>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="arbitrarentur_4">: imperf. following </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="petitum_sit_4_3">, which is regarded as a secondary tense since it represents the perf. indic. ; § <ref n="AG 485">485</ref>, a (287, a) ; B. 268, I; G. 511, N.2; H.546 (495, i); H.-B. 481. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="a_autem_4">, etc., lit. <gloss>but from <emph>me</emph> </gloss>, etc. (opposed to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="a_ceteris_4_2"> above). The emphasis may be preserved by changing the construction in English: <gloss>but as for myself, men have urged it</gloss> [i.e. that I should undertake the defence of Roscius] <gloss>on me who</gloss>, etc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ei_4">,<gloss> men</gloss> ; here used simply as a correlative to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_4">, and not in a really demonstrative sense. The reference is of course to the noble friends of Roscius </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="debeam_4">, subj. of characteristic: § <ref n="AG 535">535</ref>(320); B. 283,1; G. 631, 2; H. 591, I (503, i); H.-B. 521, 1. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="his_4">, emphatic, summing up the reasons he has given for undertaking the case, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ego_4"> (next line), emphatic as opposed to the others present. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="patronus_4"> ,<gloss>advocate</gloss>, the word <foreign xml:lang="lat">advocati</foreign> having a different meaning (see note on <ref n="pl2.7">p. 2, l. 7</ref>, above). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="unus_4">,<gloss>as the one man</gloss> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="uti_ne_4">: in purpose clauses the double form is often used instead of <foreign xml:lang="lat">ne</foreign> alone. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="desertus_4">, etc.: observe that Cicero not only attempts to win the sympathies of the jurors for the helpIessness of his client, but that he also contrives to suggest, in advance of the formal statement of facts, that there is a combination or conspiracy of some kind against young Roscius The same thing was insinuated in sect. vi by the use of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="_conflatam_4"> (1.6). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hujusce_5">,<gloss>of my</gloss> client. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="municeps_Amerinus_5"> ,<gloss>a citizen of the free town Ameria</gloss>. The Latin uses an adj. of possession when it can, often where the English prefers <gloss> of</gloss>: §<ref n="AG 343">343</ref>, a (190); B. 354, 4; G. 362, R.1; H. (395, N.2). Cf. <ref n="Verg. A. 2.55">Aeneid, 2. 55</ref>,<ref n="Verg. A. 2.487">487</ref>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hospitiis_5"> ,<gloss> guest-friendships</gloss>. The <foreign xml:lang="lat"> hospitium</foreign> was a relation between individuals of different cities or states, at a time when there were no international relations ; it included the duties of hospitality and protection, was transmitted from father to son, and was vouched for by a ticket (<foreign xml:lang="lat">tessera</foreign>). Roscius not only had this formal relation to several of the greatest families at Rome, but he was also on intimate terms of personal friendship with them. Hence, in line 27, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="domesticus_consuetudo_5"> ,<gloss>intercourse and companionship</gloss> [with them] <gloss>in their homes</gloss>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="honestatis_gratia_5"> (so </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="honoris_causa_5">, §<ref n="AG 17">17</ref> ),<gloss>with all honor</gloss>. It seems to have been held a liberty to mention the name of any person of quality in a public address ; hence such mention is generally accompanied by a form of compliment. Cf. the modern parliamentary usage of referring to members of a deliberative body by the names of their offices (or as the "gentleman from ") rather than by their own names. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hoc_solum_5">, i.e. the <foreign xml:lang="lat">hospitium</foreign> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="domestici_5">, of his own house. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ereptum_possident_5"> ,<gloss>have seized and now hold</gloss>: §<ref n="AG 496">496</ref>, N.2 (292, R.) ; G. 664, R.1; H.639 (549,5); H.-B. 604, 1 ; <foreign xml:lang="lat"> possidere</foreign> does not signify <gloss>to own</gloss>, in the modern sense, but merely <gloss>to hold</gloss> or <gloss>occupy</gloss> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="innocentis_5">, i.e. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fili_5">: in Latin any noun may be left out if there is an adj. or a participle to determine its case. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="defenditur_5">: Cicero skillfully contrives to keep before the jury the fact that Roscius has powerful friends who desire his acquittal. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_6">,<gloss> when</gloss>, introducing the general situation ; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tum_6">, the particular circumstance. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="omni_tempore_6"> ,<gloss>at all times</gloss>, as opposed to the time of the Civil War: notice the emphatic position. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hoc_tumultu_6"> ,<gloss>this last disturbance</gloss> (euphemistic): i.e. the final scenes of the Civil War of Marius and Sulla, which Cicero will not call <foreign xml:lang="lat"> bellum</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_6_2">,<gloss>at a time when</gloss> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_veniret_6"> (subj. of characteristic, not simply cum temporal), was at stake. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="rectum_6">: render <gloss>no more than right</gloss> (thus giving the emphasis of its position). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="se_pugnare_6_2"> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="honestissimus_6"> refer respectively to the rank and dignity of these great families and the credit which his connection with them gave him in his own neighborhood. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="victoria_6">, i.e. of Sulla's party. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="proscriberentur_6">: the number of the proscribed in Sulla's time was 4,700. "Whoever killed one of these outlaws was not only exempt from punishment, like an executioner duly fulfilling his office, but also obtained for the execution a compensation of 22,000 <foreign xml:lang="lat">denarii</foreign> (nearly $2400); any one, on the contrary, who befriended an outlaw, even his nearest relative, was liable to the severest punishment. The property of the proscribed was forfeited to the state, like the spoil of an enemy ; their children and grandchildren were excluded from a political career, and yet, so far as of senatorial rank, were bound to undertake their share of senatorial burdens." (Mommsen.) At first only the names of those who had justly forfeited their lives were proscribed ; afterwards it became easy for friends and favorites of the dictator (like Chrysogonus, attacked in this oration) to put upon the list the names of innocent men, and even of men already dead, so as to work confiscation of their property. Sulla's proscriptions nominally ceased June 1, <date when="-0081">B.C. 81</date>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="erat_Romae_6">: this shows that he had no reason to fear the proscription. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="frequens_6">: §<ref n="AG 290">290</ref> (191); B. 239; G. 325, R.6; H. 443 (497); H.-B. 245. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_videretur_6">, clause of result. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Inimicitiae_7"> ,<gloss>causes</gloss> or <gloss>occasions of enmity</gloss>: for the plur., see § <ref n="AG 100">100</ref>, C (75, c) ; B. 55, 4, c; G. 204, N.5 ; H. 138, 2 (130, 2); H.-B. 240, 5, b. By this sentence Cicero suggests to the jury what he afterwards develops in the argument, - that a motive for the murder existed in the case of the Titi Roscii. He thus prepares the way for the elaborate countercharge (omitted in this book) made against these two later in the oration. Observe the emphasis that comes from the juxtaposition of sects. <ref n="Cic. S. Rosc. 6">6</ref> and <ref n="Cic. S. Rosc. 7">7</ref>: Roscius had nothing to fear from the proscription. <gloss>He had</gloss> <emph>enemies</emph>, however, - the very men who are now prosecuting his son. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="accusatorum_7">: prosecutions might be brought by private persons (as by Cicero against Verres). In this instance these two Roscii were associated with Erucius as prosecutors. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hujusce_7">,<gloss>of my client</gloss>- (see note on sect. <ref n="Cic. S. Rosc. 9">9</ref>, below). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="isti_7">, i.e. of the party of prosecution. <foreign xml:lang="lat">Iste</foreign>, the so-called "demonstrative of the second person," is regularly used of one's opponent in a suit or debate, as <foreign xml:lang="lat">hic</foreign> is used of one's client. See § <ref n="AG 297">297</ref>, a, c (102, a, c) ; B. 87 ; G.306; H. 505(450); cf. H.-B. 271, a. </gloss>

H.-B. 388, a: i.e. he must have been a mere apprentice (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tiro_7">) at the trade: "this is the first of his actual murders that I know of." For mood, see § <ref n="AG 535">535</ref>, d (320, d) ; B. 283, 2 ; G. 627, R.1 ; H.591, 3 (503, 1, N.1) ; H.-B. 521, 1, f. (Passages in brackets in the text are thought to be spurious insertions.) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hic_8">,<gloss>this man</gloss> (with a gesture), i.e. here at my side (my client) ; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="iste_8">,<gloss>that man</gloss>, i.e. there on the accusers' bench (Magnus). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_esset_8">: parenthetical (repeating, in greater detail, the clause that precedes). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="iste_8_2">: T. Roscius Magnus ; the repetition of the words </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="frequens_8">, etc., emphasizes the suggestion that he was likeliest to be the murderer. </gloss>

The speed with which the one sent the news to the other was, of course, suspicious, as well as the further proceedings described in sects. <ref n="Cic. S. Rosc. 10">10</ref> and <ref n="Cic. S. Rosc. 11">11</ref>, including the proscription and the sale of the property. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="inimici_9">: cf. the same word in lines 7 and 8. The reason for thus harping on the <gloss> inimicitiae</gloss> mentioned in sect. <ref n="Cic. S. Rosc. 7">7</ref>, above, must be evident. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="horam_primam_9">: the night from sunset to sunrise was divided into twelve hours. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nocturnis_9">: the travelling would be more difficult and slow in the night, though the night hours would be longer than the day hours in the late autumn or winter, when the murder is thought to have been committed. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cisiis_9">: the plural form shows that there were <gloss>relays of carriages</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quadriduo_10">, etc.: we should say <gloss>within four days from the time when</gloss>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_castra_10">: the idea of motion, vividly conceived, suggests the <gloss>acc</gloss>. of place as well as person ; we should say "<emph>to</emph> Chrysogonus <emph> in</emph> Sulla's camp <emph>at V.</emph>"; §<ref n="AG 428">428</ref>, j (259, h)<gloss/>; B. 182, 2, b; G. 337, R.6; H.-B. 450, c. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Volaterras_10">. "Here some of the Etruscans and of those proscribed by Sulla made a stand and were blockaded for two years, and then surrendered on terms." </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="defertur_10">: this word implies an intentional conveying of the information, as if in the manner of a formal report. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tris_10"> =<foreign xml:lang="lat">tres</foreign>: the acc. termination in -<foreign xml:lang="lat">is</foreign> remained in this and a few other words for a considerable time after the form in -<foreign xml:lang="lat">es</foreign> became the more common. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Tiberim_10">: the nearness of the river facilitated both irrigation and transportation, and so added much to the value of the estates. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nullo_negotio_10"> ,<gloss>without any difficulty</gloss>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vel_11"> (emphasizing the superlative), <gloss>the very</gloss> , etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="omnia_justa_13"> ,<gloss>all the due rites of burial</gloss> these ended with a sacrifice on the ninth day (<foreign xml:lang="lat">novemdialia</foreign>) after the death or burial ;</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="decurionum_15">: these constituted the municipal senate or city council. The <foreign xml:lang="lat">decem primi</foreign> were a standing executive committee of the town, to whom, in this instance, an unusual piece of business was entrusted. If the delegates had been a special committee appointed expressly to report the case to Sulla, Capito, one of the conspirators, would hardly have been chosen a member. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_vir_15">,<gloss>what sort of man</gloss>, i.e. especially in his political principles. The delegates were apparently to certify to the fact that Roscius had been of Sulla's party. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_velit_15">, that he will consent. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="decretum_15">: the decree was here read to the court, but it has not been preserved. Its reading must have produced considerable effect. It was not only important testimony to the innocence of the younger Roscius, but it was introduced by Cicero at such a point in the case as to repeat and confirm the summary of the plot just given. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="id_quod_15">,<gloss> as</gloss> (see note on <ref n="pl2.16">p. 2, l. 16</ref>, above). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nobilis_15">, acc. plur. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ab_peterent_15"> ,<gloss>to beg of them </gloss>: §<ref n="AG 531">531</ref>, 2 (317, 2); B. 282, ; G. 630; H.590 (497,i); H.-B. 502,2; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eis_15"> refers to the </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="decem_primi_15"> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ne_15"> . . .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adirent_15">, obj. of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="peterent_15"> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vellent_15">: §<ref n="AG 580">580</ref> (336, 2); B. 314, I; G. 508, 2; H. 643 (524); H.-B. 534, 2. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pollicerentur_15">, same constr. as </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="peterent_15_2"> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="antiqui_16">,<gloss>of the old stamp</gloss>, i.e. plain, honest men. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ex_natura_16"> ,<gloss>after their own nature</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ceteros_16">, subj. of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="esse_16"> understood, depending on </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fingerent_16"> ,<gloss>imagined</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="confirmaret_16"> ,<gloss>assured them</gloss> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="re_inorata_16"> ,<gloss>without having stated their case</gloss>: the primary meaning of <foreign xml:lang="lat">oro</foreign> implies not <gloss>entreaty</gloss>, but <gloss>statement</gloss> or <gloss> argument</gloss> (cf. orator). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="reverterunt_16">: the active form of this verb is found only in the tenses of the perfect stem; otherwise it is deponent. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="isti_16">, i.e. Chrysogonus and Capito. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="lentius_16">,<gloss>less energetically</gloss>. (On account of the natural correlation of opposites, it is often convenient to translate adjectives and adverbs by the negative of their contraries.) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nihil_agere_16">, i.e. refrain from action. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="deludere_16">, [and thus] <gloss>to make fools of</gloss> the Amerians (by having rendered their whole embassy in effectual). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="id_quod_16">, etc., <gloss>as we may easily infer</gloss> this point is an inference not, like the rest, an attested fact. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="neque_16">.<gloss>and . . not</gloss>, the negative qualifying </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="posse_16">:<gloss>and judge that they can no longer</gloss>, etc. In English the negative is placed near the verb; in Latin it is attracted by the connective, and so often stands at the beginning of the clause. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="domino_incolumi_16"> (abl. abs.), <gloss>so long as the owner was alive</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hic_17">,<gloss>my client</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="de_17">,<gloss>in accordance with</gloss> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cognatorum_17"> ,<gloss>blood-relations</gloss>: these were accustomed to hold a <gloss> consilium</gloss>, or formal deliberation, on important family affairs,—like the modern 'family council' of the French. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Caeciliam_17">: see sect. <ref n="Cic. S. Rosc. 50">50</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="honoris_causa_17">: cf. note on sect. <ref n="Cic. S. Rosc. 5">5</ref>,<ref n="pl3.28">p. 3, l. 28</ref>, above. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qua_plurimum_17"> ,<gloss>whose especial friendship his father had enjoyed</gloss>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Id_quod_17">, etc., i.e. she showed on this occasion (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nunc_17">) the generous traits which everybody supposed she possessed </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quasi_causa_17"> <gloss>as if to serve as a model</gloss>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="antiqui_offici_17"> ,<gloss>old-fashioned fidelity</gloss>:</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="officium_17"> means the performance of duties as well as the duties themselves </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="domo_17">, without the prep., while </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="bonis_17"> requires </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ex__17">§<ref n="AG 42">42</ref>;7 I (258, a); B. 229, 2, b; G. 390, 2; H.462, 4 (412, ii i) H-B 451 a </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vivus_referretur_17"> ,<gloss>brought alive to trial, rather than murdered and put on the proscription list</gloss> §<ref n="AG 5">5</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_pugnarent_18">: subst. clauses of purpose in app. with consilium, 1.6; § <ref n="AG 561">561</ref>, a,563 (331, headnote); Cf. B. 295; G. 546, N.2; H. 564 (499, 3); H.-B. 319 c. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nomen_deferrent_18">, i.e. lay a formal charge before the president of the proper court. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="de_parricidio_18">: §<ref n="AG 353"> 353</ref>,2 (220, b, 2); G. 378, R.2; H.456,3 (410, ii, 3); H.-B. 342, b. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="veterem_18">,<gloss> old</gloss> in the trade: the reign of terror through which Rome had just passed had given ample practice. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="de_re_18">, etc., <gloss>in</gloss> a <gloss>case in which</gloss>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="posset_18">, clause of purpose, rather than result (but the two constructions approach each other so closely that it is not always possible to distinguish between them). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="subesset_18">, subj. of char. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="suspicio_18">, i.e. should be able, from his skill as a prosecutor, to make a show of a case even when there was <gloss>no ground for suspicion</gloss> against the accused. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="crimine_18"> (abl. of means), <gloss>on the charge</gloss> itself, i.e. by any strength in the incriminating evidence. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="poterant_18">: indicative as being their reason given by Cicero on his own authority; § <ref n="AG 540"> 540</ref> (321); B. 286, 1; G. 540; H.588, 1 (516, i); H.-B. 554, 555. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tempore_18"> (opposed to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="crimine_18_2"> ),<gloss>the circumstances of the times</gloss> (i.e. partly the generally disturbed condition of the state, partly the fact that the courts were now first reopened, after their reorganization by Sulla). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="loqui_18">: historical infin.; § <ref n="AG 463">463</ref> (275); B. 335; G. 647; H.610 (536,1); H.-B. 595. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tam_diu_18">, i.e. during the Civil War. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eum_18">,<gloss>the man</gloss> (i.e. any one). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="oportere_18"> ,<gloss>was sure to</gloss> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_primus_18">: this was the first case that came before the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Quaestio inter Sicarios</foreign> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adductus_esset_18">: for fut. perf. of direct disc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="huic_18">: the emphatic position may be rendered by <gloss>in <emph>his</emph> case</gloss>. B 14 </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="gratiam_18">,<gloss> favor</gloss> or <gloss> influence</gloss>, i.e. with Sulla. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fore_ut_18">, etc.: the usual periphrasis for the fut. infin. pass.; the supine with <foreign xml:lang="lat">iri</foreign> is rare. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nullo_negotio_18">: cf. § <ref n="Cic. S. Rosc. 10">10</ref>,<ref n="pl5.15">p. 5, l. 15</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tolleretur_18">: cf. <foreign xml:lang="lat">de medio tolli</foreign>, §<ref n="Cic. S. Rosc. 10">10</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nullo_18">: for the abl. of <foreign xml:lang="lat">nemo</foreign>, which is never used. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="atque_adeo_18"> ,<gloss>or rather</gloss> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quem_18">: the antecedent is </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eum_18_2"> below. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jugulandum_18">, i.e. for judicial murder: § <ref n="AG 500">500</ref>, 4 (294, d); B. 337, 7, b, 2; G. 430; H. 622 (544, N.2); H.-B. 605, 2. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="querar_19">, deliberative subj.: § <ref n="AG 444">444</ref> (268); B. 277; G. 265; H. 559, 4 (484, v); H.-B. 503. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="unde_19">,<gloss> where</gloss>, lit. <gloss> whence</gloss>: the Latin conceives the speaker as proceeding <gloss> from</gloss> some point, whereas the English represents him as beginning <gloss> at</gloss> some point. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="potissimum_19"> (superl. of <foreign xml:lang="lat">potius</foreign>, as if <gloss> rathest</gloss>),<gloss> best</gloss> (rather than anywhere else); cf. sect. <ref n="Cic. S. Rosc. 1">1</ref>, 1.3. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="summam_potestatem_19"> ,<gloss>unlimited power</gloss> (i.e. with respect to rendering a verdict). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fidem_19">, i.e. the <gloss>protection</gloss> required by good faith. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pater_19">, etc.: these nominatives are in no grammatical construction, but are used to enumerate in a vivid way the crimes of the conspirators afterwards referred to by his (l. 26); cf. § <ref n="AG 497">497</ref> (292, a); B. 337, 5; G. 664, R.2; H. 636, 4 (549, N.2); H.-B. 608, 2. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="infesta_19">,<gloss> imperilled</gloss> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nefariis_19">, abl of instr. after </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cumulant_19">: the idea in Latin is that of making a heap of what already exists, by means of other things piled on it (hence acc. and abl.); but translate, <gloss>upon these they heap up other infamies</gloss>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hujusce_19"> (emphatic instead of <foreign xml:lang="lat">ejus</foreign>): translate by <gloss>his own</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="condicionem_19"> ,<gloss>terms</gloss> (or <gloss>dilemma</gloss>): as containing the idea of a bargain, it is followed by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_19">; §<ref n="AG 563">563</ref>, d (331, d); cf. B. 295,4; G. 546, N.2; H.564, iii (498, 1); H.-B. 502, 3, a. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cervices_19">: this word is used by early writers in the plural only. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="insutus_culeum_19">: the old punishment for a parricide was to be "beaten with blood-red rods, then sewed into a sack, with a dog, a cock, a viper, and an ape, and thrown into the deep sea" (see below, sect. <ref n="Cic. S. Rosc. 29">29</ref> ).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="patronos_19">: Cicero's modesty will not allow him to call himself a <gloss>patronus</gloss> (cf. note on <ref n="pl3.17">p. 3, l. 17</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_dicat_19">, purpose-clause: the antecedent is the subject of deest, below. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quantum_20">,<gloss>so far as</gloss> (adverbial acc.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_est_20"> ?<gloss>how then</gloss> ?</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="primo_tempore_20"> ,<gloss>the very first opportunity</gloss> (i.e that which the present case affords) since the violence and disorder of the Civil War. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="exstinguere_debetis_20">: the courts had just been restored by Sulla after a long interval of lawlessness, and the case of Roscius was the first to come before the reorganized <foreign xml:lang="lat">Quaestio inter Sicarios</foreign>. There was a general feeling that the courts ought to do something at once, a feeling that might well be prejudicial to the defendant even though he was innocent. To remove this prejudice Cicero (1) suggests that the conspirators relied on it in bringing their iniquitous charge (sect. <ref n="Cic. S. Rosc. 18">18</ref>:</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ita_esset_20">), and (2) shows that an acquittal, by rebuking the effrontery and violence of men like Chrysogonus and his confederates, would do much to restore law and order. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ejus_modi_21"> ,</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quo_maleficio_21"> ,<gloss>of such a kind, that in this one crime</gloss> (rel. clause of result). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="voltu_21">,<gloss>by a look</gloss>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="postularet_21">, . . .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cogebant_21"> ,<gloss>would compel it if the case required</gloss>: see § <ref n="AG 517">517</ref>, b (308, b); cf. B. 304, 3; G. 597, R.3; H. 583, I (5 </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jura_cogebant_21"> is equivalent to a verb of necessity, and hence the imperf. indic. in the apodosis appears with the imperf. subj. in the protasis. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="auditum_sit_21">, a general condition; subj. because integral part of the result clause. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tu_21"> (emphatic), <gloss>you</gloss>, a professional prosecutor. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="censes_21">: the word used to express deliberate judgment, after discussion or the like. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mores_21">,<gloss> character</gloss>, as resulting from habits of life; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="naturam_21"> (next line), <gloss>natural disposition</gloss>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tu_21_2">: emphatic, as opposed to the general run of accusers. Cicero is here using the famous "argument from probability," a favorite with ancient orators and rhetoricians from the fifth century B.C. "For example, if a physically weak man be accused of an assault, he is to ask the jury, 'Is it <emph>probable</emph> that a weakling like me should have attacked anybody?' while if the accused is a strong man he is to claim that it is <emph> improbable</emph> that he should have committed an assault in a case where his strength was sure to be used as a presumption against him." </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="patrem_22">, etc.: to preserve the emphasis we may render a <gloss> <emph>parricide</emph> has been committed by Sex. Roscius</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_homo_22"> ?<gloss>what sort of man</gloss> (is it who has committed such a crime) ? </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adulescentulus_22">: the diminutive suggests a weak stripling led astray (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="_inductus_22">) ; the defendant was, in fact, a man of forty. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nequam_22">, with hominibus. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="major_22">: anomalous for the more usual <foreign xml:lang="lat">plus</foreign> or <foreign xml:lang="lat">amplius</foreign>; §<ref n="AG 407">407</ref>, c (247, c); B. 217, 3; G. 311, R.4; H.471, 4 (417, I, N.2) ; H.-B 416, d. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vetus_22"> (emphatic), <gloss>old</gloss> (in the sense of the English derivative <gloss> inveterate</gloss>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="videlicet_22"> ,<gloss>no doubt, of course</gloss>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="de_luxuria_22">: for constr. see note on </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="de_parricidio_22"> (<ref n="pl8.7">p. 8, l. 7</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cuiquam_22">: words in italics are not in the manuscripts, but are supplied by modern scholars (from conjecture) as being necessary to the construction or the sense. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="objecit_22">: the accuser had made it a point in his argument that the defendant was of a morose temper, shunning all society and burying himself in the country. Cicero deftly turns these assertions to the advantage of his client. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quo_modo_31"> ,<gloss>how</gloss>. Cicero contends that he is not obliged to discuss the manner of the murder, since Erucius has not raised that point, and has not even been able to assign a motive. His own position in the argument is so strong, however, that, he says, he can afford to concede a point by waiving the question of <gloss>motive</gloss> and allowing Erucius to argue the case on the basis of the <gloss>means</gloss> by which Roscius could have committed the crime. This is of course a rhetorical device to introduce one of Cicero's strongest arguments. It cannot be proved that it was even possible for Roscius to kill his father under the circumstances. By calling for the details of the murder Cicero shows that none can be produced. The whole passage serves also as an effective preparation for the countercharge (omitted in this edition), in which it is shown that Sex. Roscius Magnus had not only a motive, but every opportunity. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="facio_34">) ; such phrases are often used colloquially or with emphasis, for the simple verb: § <ref n="AG 497"> 497</ref>, c (292, d) ; G. 537 ; H.-B. 605, 4. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illud_quaero_34">, THIS <gloss>is what I want to know</gloss>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="per_quos_34">: these words are the interrogative expression with which the clause grammatically begins ; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="is_homo_34"> is put first for emphasis. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="suspiciose_34">, i.e. so as to look suspicious. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_rebus_34"> ,<gloss>but in <emph> these</emph> circumstances</gloss>, i.e. those in our case (emphatic position). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="suspicio_culpam_34">: i.e. in so clear a case I will not ask Erucius for proof of guilt ; if he can show any suspicious circumstance, it shall suffice. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="credo_34">,<gloss>I suppose</gloss>: ironical, as usual when parenthetical. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="causa_dicitur_34"> ,<gloss>the defendant is on trial</gloss> (a technical term: lit. <gloss>the case is argued</gloss>, i.e. by the defendant). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="admiserit_35">: §<ref n="AG 569">569</ref> (332, a) ; B. 297, 2 ; G. 553, 4; H. 571, I (501, i, vi); H.-B. 521, 3, a, footnote 2 </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_35">,<gloss> that</gloss>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_35_2">: the antecedent is id (<ref n="pl15.1">p. 15, l. 1</ref>); the clause </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_polliceatur_35"> is in apposition with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_35_3">.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quaestionem_35"> ,<gloss>question</gloss>, in the technical sense, i.e. examination by torture, the regular legal way of examining slaves. An accused person could, of his own accord, offer his slaves for that purpose (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="_polliceri_35">): in this Case Roscius had lost his slaves, and so was deprived of that privilege. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="unus_puer_35"> ,<gloss>as much as a single slave</gloss>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="minister_35">, i.e. to wait upon him. - familia: this word, in its primary meaning, properly embraced the entire body of free persons, Clients, and slaves, under the patriarchal rule of the <gloss>paterfamilias</gloss>. In time, the meaning was divided, applying either (1) to the family proper — the <gloss> paterfamilias</gloss>, with his wife, children, etc. ; or (2) to a body (or gang) of slaves. The latter is the meaning here. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Scipio_35">,</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Metelle_35">: these were, probably, P. Scipio Nasica, father of Metellus Scipio (a leader on Pompey's side in the Civil War), and his cousin, Q. Metellus Nepos, brother of Caecilia (sect. <ref n="Cic. S. Rosc. 50">50</ref>), and father of the Celer and Nepos referred to in the orations against Catiline. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="advocatis_35"> ,<gloss>called in</gloss> (as friends of the accused) ; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="agentibus_35"> ,<gloss>taking active part</gloss>. The demand seems to have been formal, and these friends were present to attest it. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="meministisne_35"> : </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ne_35"> =</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nonne_35">; §<ref n="AG 332">332</ref>, c (210, d); B. 162, 2, c; G. 454, N.5 ; H.-B. 231, b, N.1. - T. Roscium, i.e. Magnus. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sectantur_35"> ,<gloss>are in the train of</gloss> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_facitis_35">: up to this point Cicero appears to be merely accounting for the fact (which might have made against his case) that the younger Roscius had not offered his slaves for examination. With this abrupt question he shows the true bearing of the refusal of Magnus, retorting suddenly the countercharge, which he carries out in a chapter here omitted. The effect on a jury of such an appeal as Dubitate, etc., must have been very great. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aureum_36">: the Greek name Chrysogonus means <gloss>gold-born</gloss>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="latuit_36">: because his was the only name that appeared. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="alii_quoque_36">, i.e. other purchasers of confiscated estates. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_mihi_36">, etc., i.e. I have no occasion to say anything of the purchasers of confiscated estates in general, for this case, by its atrocity, is taken out of the common category (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="haec_causa_36">, etc., 1.24, below). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sectorum_36">: these were the purchasers of confiscated property in the lump, who afterwards <gloss> divided</gloss> it (<foreign xml:lang="lat">seco</foreign>) to sell again in detail. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="venierunt_37">, from <foreign xml:lang="lat"> veneo</foreign> not <foreign xml:lang="lat">venio</foreign>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_haec_37"> ,<gloss>for if such remarks</gloss>, etc.. i.e. if I may be allowed to speak freely. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tantus_homo_37"> ,<gloss>such a great-person</gloss>: a hint that more important men than he had suffered. In fact, all the really eminent victims of the Civil War had perished before the proscription. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_37"> (adv.), <gloss>how</gloss>?</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Valeria_37">: the law by which Sulla was made perpetual dictator and invested with absolute power of life and death (<date when="-0082">B.C. 82</date>); it was proposed by L. Valerius Flaccus as <foreign xml:lang="lat">interrex</foreign>. Laws were designated by the <gloss> gentile</gloss> name of their proposer ; all laws, for example, carried by L. Cornelius Sulla were known as <foreign xml:lang="lat">Leges Corneliae</foreign>.<gloss/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Cornelia_37">: this appears to have been enacted some time after the <foreign xml:lang="lat">lex Valeria</foreign>, in order to regulate the details of the proscription. Cicero's ignorance of the law is no doubt affected. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="novi_37">, I know the thing or person ; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="scio_37">,<gloss>I know the fact</gloss> :<gloss>I am not acquainted with the law, and do not know which it is</gloss> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="proscripti_sunt_37">: the indic. must mean those already proscribed when the law was passed. Future cases of proscription would have been referred to by the subj. or fut. perf. (see Verr. ii, chs. xli, xlii). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_praesidiis_37"> ,<gloss>among the armed forces</gloss>, etc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dum_37">,<gloss>so long as</gloss>: §<ref n="AG 556">556</ref>, ? (276, e, N.) ; G. 569; H. 603, i (519, i) ; H.-B. 550 and b. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="veteres_37">, those of the regular code; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="novas_37">, those of the Sullan revolution. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="occisum_37"> esse, indir. disc. with constat ; the subject accusative is omitted. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_eum_38">, i.e. Sulla Here it is necessary for the orator to proceed with great caution: even if not himself present, Sulla would watch sharply the first case before his own criminal court. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ab_initio_38"> ,<gloss>from the beginning</gloss> of this trial (see sect. <ref n="Cic. S. Rosc. 12">12</ref> );</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="omni_tempore_38">, throughout his whole career. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_ementiretur_38">, . . .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="passus_sit_38">, clauses in appos. with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="haec_omnia_38">: for the change of tense, see § <ref n="AG 475">475</ref>, a (279, d). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="apud_adversarios_38"> ,<gloss>in the enemy's ranks</gloss> (=</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_praesidiis_38">, above). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="postea_38">: the passage referred to appears to have been lost out of the oration, probably in the gap in ch. xlv. The scholiast represents Chrysogonus as saying that he had used the property in building a villa at Veii. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="diem_39">.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tabulas_39">: confiscated property belonged to the state, and public records of its seizure and sale were, of course, kept. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nulla_39">,<gloss>not at all</gloss>: §<ref n="AG 290">290</ref> (191); B. 239; G. 325, R.6; H. 513, 3 (457, 3); H.-B. 245. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="redierunt_39"> =<foreign xml:lang="lat">relata sunt</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="facetius_39"> ,<gloss>more cleverly</gloss>: in the case supposed, the pretended proscription would never have occurred and the property would have been taken without even the forms of law. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ante_tempus_39"> ,<gloss>too early</gloss>, i.e. before it is time to raise so trivial a question as that of a title to property (Roscius is now on trial for his life). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="reduviam_curem_39"> (proverbial), <gloss>treat a sore finger</gloss>, i e. in a case of life and death I deal only with some trifling ailment. For mood see § <ref n="AG 535">535</ref>, e (320, e); B. 283, 3; G. 586 H. 592 (517); H.-B. 523. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_ducit_39"> ,<gloss>he does not take account</gloss> (a mercantile phrase). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="partim_me_40"> ,<gloss>partly in my own name</gloss>. To avoid entangling the case of his client with politics, Cicero makes himself responsible for everything that may have a political bearing ; he was a well-known partisan of the nobility and could afford to speak freely. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quaeque_40">: not from </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quisque_40">. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_pertinere_40"> ,<gloss>concerns all</gloss>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sensu_dolore_40"> ,<gloss>feeling</gloss> <gloss>and pain</gloss>, i.e. painful feeling (so-called hendiadys). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jam_40">, with the fut., <gloss> presently</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ego_41">, opposed to Roscio. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="diem_41">: fem.; § <ref n="AG 97">97</ref>, a (73); B. 53; G. 64; H.135(123); H.-B. 101. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praefinita_41"> ,<gloss>fixed in advance</gloss>, as the limit (<foreign xml:lang="lat"> finio</foreign>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="patronum_41">, i.e. Sulla see note on </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="libertum_41"> ,<ref n="pl6.10">p. 6, l. 10</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="conferre_41"> ,<gloss>throw the responsibility for</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="egerit_41">,<gloss>will effect</gloss>, fut. perf. for fut.: § <ref n="AG 516">516</ref>, C, N. (307, c, R.) ; G. 244; H. 540 (473); cf. H.-B. 490. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="imprudente_41">: cf. <ref n="pl5.28">p. 5, l. 28</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="placet_42">,<gloss>do I like</gloss> ? i.e. <gloss>do I think it right</gloss>? </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="imprudentia_42"> ,<gloss>want of foresight</gloss>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="etenim_si_42">, etc. (the apod. is </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_miramur_42"> ,<ref n="pl17.32">p. 17, l. 32</ref>). The comparison that follows is perhaps somewhat strained ; but it accords with the habits of thought of the ancients, to whom the powers of a supreme ruler appeared in a manner divine. (Cf. the language used of Caesar in the Oration for Marcellus.) The tone in which kings were addressed in modern literature until very recent times may be compared (see, e.g., Bacon's dedication of his <gloss>Advancement of Learning</gloss> to James I). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pernicii_42">, for <foreign xml:lang="lat"> perniciei</foreign>: §<ref n="AG 98">98</ref>, N. (74, a); B. 52, 2; G. 63, N.1; H. 134, 2 and 3 (121, 1); cf. H.-B. 100, 3. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vi_rerum_42"> ,<gloss>by the very violence of the elements</gloss>, - the agents or powers which he has to control. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_42"> is causal, but may be translated <gloss>when</gloss>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nisi_42">, here as often (more commonly with <foreign xml:lang="lat">forte</foreign> or <foreign xml:lang="lat">vero</foreign>) introducing a <foreign xml:lang="lat">reductio ad absurdum</foreign>: §<ref n="AG 525">525</ref>, b and N. (315, b arid N.) ; G. 591, R.4; H.-B. 578.3, a. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_42">, pron.: the anteced. is </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="id_42">.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="possit_42">,</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adepta_sit_42">, informal indir. disc., as expressing the thought of the person surprised: § <ref n="AG 592">592</ref> (341); B. 323; G. 662; H. 649. i (528); H.-B. 535, 1, a. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_sit_42">, clause with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mirum_42">: §<ref n="AG 572">572</ref>, b, N. (333, R.) ; G. 542, N.1. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vereor_43">: for emphat. position cf. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="credo_43">, p.2, l. 1. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="imperitior_43">: §<ref n="AG 291">291</ref>, a (93, a) ; B. 240, 1 ; G. 297, 2; H. 498 (444, 1); H.-B. 241, 2. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tametsi_43">,<gloss>and yet</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="meo_jure_43"> ,<gloss>with perfect right</gloss> -(as belonging to that party) ; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jure_43"> alone would mean justly ; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="meo_43"> limits it to the speaker's own case. The passage that follows is interesting, as showing the way in which Cicero regarded the general principles at stake in the Civil War, and the excesses of the victorious party. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pro_mea_43">, etc., <gloss>to the extent of my poor and feeble ability</gloss>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_componeretur_43"> ,<gloss>that reconciliation should be made</gloss>: a clause of result in appos. with id: § <ref n="AG 567">567</ref>, 568 (332 and headnote) ; B. 297 and 3; G. 557; H. 571,4 (501, iii); H.-B. 521,3,a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_vicerunt_43"> ,<gloss>who did</gloss> (in fact) <gloss>conquer</gloss>: the subj. here would mean, whatever party <gloss>might</gloss> conquer: § <ref n="AG 593">593</ref>, a, N.1 (342, N.) ; G. 629, E. ; H. 652, I (529, ii, N.1). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="humilitatem_43">, not merely <gloss>low rank</gloss>, but meanness and vulgarity ; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dignitate_43"> ,<gloss>personal worth</gloss>, from birth and services ; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="amplitudine_43"> (next line), <gloss>rank</gloss> or position-prominence in the state. With all his arrogance, blood-thirstiness, and narrow conservatism, Sulla was, in fact, the representative of orderly government against anarchy and mob-law. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="perditi_erat_43"> (pred. gen.), <gloss>it was the part of a bad citizen</gloss>: §<ref n="AG 343">343</ref>, C (214, d) ; B. 198, 3; G. 366; H. 439 (401, 402); H.-B. 340. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quibus_incolumibus_43"> (abl. abs.), <gloss>by whose safety</gloss>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="retineretur_43"> ,<gloss>would be preserved</gloss>: fut. Cond., the protasis being </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quibus_incolumibus_43_2">: §<ref n="AG 516">516</ref>,f (307,f); G. 596, ; H. 575,9 (507, N.7); H.-B. 580, b. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quae_43">, i.e. the reinstating of the nobility. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="felicitate_43">: see note on <ref n="pl6.7">p. 6, l. 7</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_est_44"> (impers.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_eos_44">,<gloss>that those have been punished</gloss> (a euphemistic expression for the proscription). Observe the chiastic order of ideas ; (a) the punishment ; (b) the persons on whom it was inflicted; (b1) the persons rewarded; (a1) the reward. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quae_44">, referring to both the punishment and the reward just spoken of. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_partium_44"> ,<gloss>in favor of that party</gloss>:</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="studium_44"> is the regular word for siding with a particular party. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="id_est_44"> ,<gloss>this was the object</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="idcirco_44">, antecedent to the purpose clause. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ornabitur_44">. Nothing can exceed the skill with which, throughout this oration, Cicero keeps before the minds of the jury the distinction between the great cause of Sulla and the nobility and the unscrupulous greed of some of Sulla's partisans. His continual allusions to his client's hereditary friendships with the aristocracy have this end, among others, in view. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="male_45">: to <gloss>speak ill</gloss> is to <gloss>utter abuse</gloss> or <gloss>calumny</gloss>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="causam_communicare_45"> ,<gloss>identify their cause with that of</gloss> etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="equestrem_45">, referring to the struggle for the <foreign xml:lang="lat">judicia</foreign> and the extensive sympathy of the <foreign xml:lang="lat">equites</foreign> with the party of Marius. <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="servi_45">: Chrysogonus had been Sulla's slave. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fidem_45">, etc., <gloss>your honor</gloss> (good faith), <gloss>your oath, and your courts</gloss> ; i.e. after getting possession of political power, these low-born fellows were now aiming at the courts, the one security of public faith and good government. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jusjurandum_45">: the jurors were under oath to give a righteous judgment. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="neque_possit_45">: Cicero does not wish to encourage him by admitting for a moment that he can really do anything in this case: it is the fact that he has dared to hope to accomplish something that is an outrage. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="verear_45">: subj. because it expresses not a real reason, but one introduced for the sole purpose of being contradicted: § <ref n="AG 540">540</ref>, N.3 (321, R.); B. 286, I, b; G. 541, N.2; H. 588, ii (516, 2); H.-B. 535,2, b. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="talis_viros_45">: cf. <ref n="pl13.8">p. 13, l. 8</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="probe_novit_46">: note the strong sarcasm, which points the distinction between the noble cause which was at stake and the sordid motives of Chrysogonus. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quae_domi_52">, i.e. the personal protection of Roscius, supply of money, providing of witnesses, etc. </gloss>
